subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Conformity and Normative Social Influence (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study examined whether conformity to peer opinion influences adolescents' aesthetic judgments of visual art.
Participants
One hundred and forty-four high school students in grades 9 through 11 were recruited from two public high schools in a mid-sized city. Parental consent and student assent were obtained. Students who reported a formal background in art (enrollment in advanced art classes or participation in a competitive art program) were excluded to reduce expertise effects. The final sample consisted of 136 students (71 girls, 65 boys), with ages ranging from 14 to 17 years (mean = 15.4, standard deviation = 1.1). Racial/ethnic composition was: 43% White/European American, 24% Black/African American, 20% Hispanic/Latino, 8% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other.
Method
Participants completed the study in individual cubicles with a laptop computer. During Phase 1 (baseline), each participant rated the aesthetic appeal of 20 abstract digital paintings on a 10-point scale (1 = not at all appealing, 10 = extremely appealing). Participants were told that other students had previously rated the same paintings.
After completing baseline ratings, participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions. In the social influence condition, participants were shown fabricated peer ratings alongside the paintings. For 10 of the 20 paintings, the fabricated peer ratings were substantially higher (by approximately 3 points) than the participant's own baseline rating; for the other 10, fabricated peer ratings were substantially lower. In the control condition, participants saw no peer rating information and were asked to re-rate all 20 paintings after a brief filler task.
In Phase 2, all participants re-rated all 20 paintings. Conformity was calculated as the degree to which participants' Phase 2 ratings shifted toward the provided peer ratings relative to their Phase 1 baseline.
Results and Discussion
The researchers predicted that adolescents in the social influence condition would shift their ratings significantly more than those in the control condition. The results are summarized in the table.

Mean Conformity Shift (in points) by Condition and Direction

| Condition | Shift Toward Higher Peer Ratings | Shift Toward Lower Peer Ratings |
|---|---|---|
| Social influence condition | +1.8 | -1.6 |
| Control condition | +0.2 | -0.1 |

Participants in the social influence condition shifted their ratings significantly toward the fabricated peer ratings in both directions, while control participants showed minimal change. The researchers concluded that adolescents show substantial conformity to peer aesthetic judgments and that social influence effects are roughly symmetric — adolescents adjust their ratings upward or downward in response to peer opinion.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately identifies the research method as an experiment.

required_elements: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the social influence condition or the control condition.
alternatives.correct_example: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to the social influence condition or the control condition.

required_elements: This is an experiment because an independent variable (exposure to fabricated peer ratings) was manipulated and participants were randomly assigned to conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: This is an experiment because an independent variable (exposure to fabricated peer ratings) was manipulated and participants were randomly assigned to conditions.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of conformity as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response states that conformity was operationally defined as the degree to which participants' Phase 2 ratings shifted toward the provided peer ratings relative to their Phase 1 baseline ratings.

required_elements: Conformity was measured as the change in aesthetic rating points between Phase 1 and Phase 2 in the direction of the fabricated peer ratings.
alternatives.correct_example: Conformity was measured as the change in aesthetic rating points between Phase 1 and Phase 2 in the direction of the fabricated peer ratings.

required_elements: The operational definition of conformity is the numeric shift (in points) from baseline ratings toward the peer ratings presented during Phase 2.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition of conformity is the numeric shift (in points) from baseline ratings toward the peer ratings presented during Phase 2.

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the effect of peer ratings on adolescents' aesthetic judgments.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately describes that participants in the social influence condition shifted their ratings substantially toward the fabricated peer ratings (approximately +1.8 toward higher ratings and -1.6 toward lower ratings), while control participants showed very little change (+0.2 and -0.1), indicating that exposure to peer opinion significantly altered aesthetic judgments.

required_elements: The data show that social influence caused much larger rating shifts than the control condition — adolescents in the influence condition changed their ratings by roughly 1.6–1.8 points on average toward peer opinions.
alternatives.correct_example: The data show that social influence caused much larger rating shifts than the control condition — adolescents in the influence condition changed their ratings by roughly 1.6–1.8 points on average toward peer opinions.

required_elements: Compared to the minimal shifts in the control condition, adolescents exposed to peer ratings changed their aesthetic judgments by approximately 1.7 points on average, suggesting strong peer influence.
alternatives.correct_example: Compared to the minimal shifts in the control condition, adolescents exposed to peer ratings changed their aesthetic judgments by approximately 1.7 points on average, suggesting strong peer influence.

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers followed in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: The response identifies that both parental consent and student assent were obtained, following the ethical guideline of informed consent (with special attention to minors).

required_elements: The response identifies that the use of fabricated peer ratings constituted deception, which would require a debriefing of participants at the end of the study.
alternatives.correct_example: The response identifies that the use of fabricated peer ratings constituted deception, which would require a debriefing of participants at the end of the study.

required_elements: The response identifies that participation appeared to be voluntary (students assented individually), following ethical principles of voluntary participation.
alternatives.correct_example: The response identifies that participation appeared to be voluntary (students assented individually), following ethical principles of voluntary participation.

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students claim the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must address both a way it is and a way it is not generalizable.

scoring_points.description: The response proposes a generalizability claim with a specific population reference and supporting evidence from the study.

required_elements: The findings may generalize to high school students in grades 9–11 generally, but may not apply to adults or younger children because all participants were adolescents aged 14–17.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may generalize to high school students in grades 9–11 generally, but may not apply to adults or younger children because all participants were adolescents aged 14–17.

required_elements: Generalizability may be limited because students with formal art backgrounds were excluded, so findings may not reflect how art-trained adolescents respond to peer influence.
alternatives.correct_example: Generalizability may be limited because students with formal art backgrounds were excluded, so findings may not reflect how art-trained adolescents respond to peer influence.

required_elements: The study may generalize to high schoolers in similarly sized cities but may not apply to private school students or students from rural areas, since participants came from only two public schools in a mid-sized city.
alternatives.correct_example: The study may generalize to high schoolers in similarly sized cities but may not apply to private school students or students from rural areas, since participants came from only two public schools in a mid-sized city.

parts.prompt: Explain how normative social influence could account for the conformity observed in the social influence condition.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately explains that normative social influence refers to conforming to group opinion to gain acceptance or avoid social rejection, and that adolescents in the social influence condition may have adjusted their aesthetic ratings toward the peer consensus to fit in with or match their peer group's perceived preferences.

required_elements: Normative social influence explains why participants shifted their ratings toward peer opinions — they wanted to align with the group norm to avoid standing out or being seen as different from their peers.
alternatives.correct_example: Normative social influence explains why participants shifted their ratings toward peer opinions — they wanted to align with the group norm to avoid standing out or being seen as different from their peers.

required_elements: The adolescents conformed to the fabricated peer ratings due to normative social influence: adjusting one's expressed judgment to match the group's opinion to gain social approval.
alternatives.correct_example: The adolescents conformed to the fabricated peer ratings due to normative social influence: adjusting one's expressed judgment to match the group's opinion to gain social approval.

parts.prompt: Explain how the concept of retrieval cues could pose a limitation on the study's Phase 2 ratings for participants in the control condition.

scoring_points.description: The response accurately explains that retrieval cues are contextual factors that help activate memories, and that control participants re-rating the same 20 paintings may have been influenced by memory of their Phase 1 ratings (their prior ratings serving as retrieval cues), artificially inflating consistency in the control condition rather than reflecting stable aesthetic preferences.

required_elements: Control participants may have remembered or been cued to their Phase 1 ratings when re-rating in Phase 2, meaning the minimal shift in the control group could partly reflect retrieval of prior judgments rather than true preference stability.
alternatives.correct_example: Control participants may have remembered or been cued to their Phase 1 ratings when re-rating in Phase 2, meaning the minimal shift in the control group could partly reflect retrieval of prior judgments rather than true preference stability.

required_elements: The paintings themselves served as retrieval cues that helped control participants recall their original ratings, which could have inflated consistency in the control condition and made the difference between conditions appear larger than it truly is.
alternatives.correct_example: The paintings themselves served as retrieval cues that helped control participants recall their original ratings, which could have inflated consistency in the control condition and made the difference between conditions appear larger than it truly is.
